speaker: interviewer
text: were talking about getting little pieces of fish when you were in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I ground it up. In all [LAUGHS] these little eyes, thousands of eyes looking at me, I couldn't stand it. [LAUGHS] I took a pointed knife and I picked all these eyes. I couldn't stand them looking at me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have an apartment? Did you live with other people in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I think I-- first, I had an apartment. But then one day I come from work, and on my front door and on my back door there is a sign, a requisition. Is that the name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Requisition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tomorrow by noon time, you're not to be here. Leave your paintings, [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] not carpets-- rugs, rugs, crystals, fresh bedding, fresh bedding. When you put on change, you know. And not to be here tomorrow, noon time. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In those years, you did-- I don't know if I told you-- in those years, you know, Warsaw is a cold place. It's winter. My husband had a cousin, and they lived west, at the west side where it is close to Germany. On this side, where I lived, where I was born, there is Russian influence.

speaker: interviewer
text: On this side, south-- I mean, west of Poland, there is Germany. And so the people who lived there are under German influence. They speak German. They didn't know anything of Russian language. So, now, I told you I was mobilized to the front, didn't I? Well, let's stay with ghetto for now. You were in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, and I got very much attached to this family. They were three generations. In the peaceful time, they were business people with good monies. When they threw them out into the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when-- if you wanted to take a jacket before they take you, you go to the closet to reach for it, they'd have shot you. So people just left the way they had things on them and that's all. Now, I don't know if I told I told you, there was no soap. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You see, it came to a point that we didn't have soap. I mean, I had some, but those who were thrown into the ghetto, they couldn't take with think of soap when they couldn't think of anything more important. So they came into the ghetto. That which they wore came apart. And they were sitting around-- I not to forget [? your ?] a situation-- sitting around the ones on the sidewalk that you see sometimes homeless, that's what it is. And when you had a brown bag, like I have now, with something in it, they called them the catchers or the snatchers. They would snatch it because they hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in one situation, you're not going to believe. The German, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what they did, they wanted to show the world how bad we are, that we are so bad. So I was dressed like with a hat, and decent, like you dress. And they would-- these [NON-ENGLISH], I told you-- they would go and pick you from the street. And I had to take-- you see, the starvation was big. And who dies most? The parents, and their children are not alive. I know, because their bodies need more food or because they feel pity for the children and give their bite to them. I don't know the reason. So, they die first.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were left the children. And the children grow, and they need food and they need clothing. So I would throw away, we would throw away the feathers from the [? tick. ?] I would make them sort of like a dress, like just sleeveless, like this. Now, when they, we came to [NON-ENGLISH], you know a soldier came to my house and checked me if I have lice. And also, they told me to give them my husband's shirt. When you have lice, they plant their eggs into here. So he would come and ask me to give him a look through my hair. Didn't find anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: If they did, they would shave me, take me to the gas chamber. Not the gas chamber to kill you, but to take your clothing and put it through the Zyklon B. You know what Zyklon B is? And they would shave me. But they didn't find anything of this kind, so he came and he left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when they started to come to house, that [NON-ENGLISH] come to the houses with a whistle. All down, all come down to take you to the [NON-ENGLISH]. Wait a minute, that time, that time it was later when everybody, everybody was to be taken away. In the beginning, I stayed with my mother-in-law, because they took away my apartment and the things. When I came, she said, oh, welcome. So I took a blanket, and I packed what I could. And with that bundle, we went to his parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and your husband lived with your mother-in-law in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't live. I'll tell you the story. She said, it's all yours. You are welcome. She didn't mean it. In meantime, I told you, I was going from-- they called them like-- when they brought all those people to the ghetto, where do you place them?

speaker: interviewer
text: So they placed them in abandoned places. Like in a-- there was a laundry, [NON-ENGLISH], a laundry. So the men ran off in the-- call it, apartment, or call it-- what do you call it? A store. They placed them there. They placed them in, in, in synagogues. What about you? Where were you and your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto, where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, first I lived with my sister-in-law. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We just married, and the bombing tore out this part, a corner, where you could see the light in the outside. I forgot to tell you about the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: First, tell me were you lived. Then from when the, when this building was destroyed, where did you live then?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't destroyed, just part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you stayed there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there. Then it came to a point when they started to come and take away the people. So they, they had them come down. And they covered the whole street. And they lead them to go to [NON-ENGLISH]. My husband got there and he-- and the [NON-ENGLISH] were after him. And he understood that when he is holding to the back, he'll whisk away. And that's what he did. When they went after him, he gave them the watch. And they let him go the first time or the second time. Then they took them to the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was working in the [NON-ENGLISH], I told you, a health organization. And we were sticking around there. And when they came there to take them-- and I cannot forget this woman-- there was-- who has no papers to show to the [NON-ENGLISH], they put them outside the gate. And who had something to show, they put them-- let them go back to the building. And there was this nurse, and she had, she worked already on the [NON-ENGLISH]. I mean, she had two papers. She gave me one.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told us that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had this family. Remember, I told you. And the daughter-in-law of that family said to me, sister, wherever they take my mother-in-law, I go with her. I didn't want her to go alone. Will you look after my children? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She had in her mind a mother's heart, sick of pain, what happened to the children. She said, you take care of Annette and Simon, I think, their names. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I set out. And I had a silver coin, I told you? No. I thought, I'll go to the gate. There is a, a German guard by the gate.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Oh, they took us, quote unquote, 'to work,' sort of. It's fake. And they would-- they wanted Jewish [NON-ENGLISH]. I could choke him at night if-- he almost destroyed me. And he'd count up 10. Supposedly, they will go to work on the field south of Warsaw. The Germans had cabbage to be weeded out, and something. So, we went to go. And I had-- I met a friend who was a nurse. And I said to her, you see, we're going to perish. She said, you are free. Your husband is taken away. You can go. I can not go. I can not. Her, her husband was a doctor of stomatology. I am not going to leave him behind. And she didn't look Jewish at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. You mentioned your husband was taken away. When was your husband taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine? August 11th, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where he was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Treblinka. At that time, they were taking people to Treblinka. Later on, they stared to take them to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was going on in Treblinka at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did know. Sure, I did. They gassed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that then, that that's what was happening to people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I did know. I don't think I did know.

speaker: interviewer
text: In order to get in and out of the ghetto, you had to have-- either go with a work force or have a permit. Was the ghetto closed?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, you don't call it closed. No. Closed wasn't '39. In '42 it was locked, I mean. In '39, you can go in and out. Did I tell you about being Yom Kippur?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, a friend of mine, this nurse, my sister's friend, she called me and I cried. And I said, they took away my husband, and now I am to be the next. And she says, no, you come. I said, where? She says, come to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: How would I go? She says, I'll send you a policeman, and you'll pay him-- I think I forgot now-- some money. I forgot now. And he'll take you out. But I knew better. I knew the policeman will take you and give you straight away to kill. I decided no.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I met this nurse, this friend-- another nurse, not this one-- and she said, there are underground passages. My husband, my husband knows where they are. If you come after Sabbath prayer, he'll show you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here's how it goes. You get-- it's a basement. You get on the steps, and you go under, under, under, under. It's a big building. It was a big, brand new court. The first time when I went there, I met there-- you see, my sister-in-law, her husband was bringing thread from Russia-- I don't know, I forgot where-- and sell it here. So she gave me a spool. Everything was-- gold was much cheaper than anything that's of use. She gave me this thread. And I was thinking much about my sister. She has no money. So her boyfriend, who was a Polish doctor, we met in that court building, and I gave him the thread for her to sell and have for daily bread. Now, then came Yom Kippur, and I got a call from her friend, that nurse, and she said, I have a surprise for you. But those years we wouldn't talk on the phone, because we knew they listen in. We didn't talk. She said, I have a surprise for you. If you come to me I'll show you. It was Yom Kippur day. My, my husband was going to pray, and I decided I am going to see her. That time when they catch you, they put you only for a half year in jail-- that time, the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, my husband said, if you go you'd be on your own. When they take you to jail, I'm not going to bail you out. Stay here. If you risk, you risk on your own. I'm not going to bail you out. He went to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: I packed [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] fish, and I set out to go. But I had the Jewish face. So, I had a navy blue felt hat, so I put that hat like on this side is the rim up, on this side it's down to cover me. And I'm walking, and the police-- end of tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, you were talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I-- first time when I left ghetto, I went underground. There were these little boys. That was a path, so to say, for the smugglers. They would smuggle into the ghetto food, and take out clothing, bedding, whatever they had a hand on, because everything was very expensive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they would smuggle a path, path. And when the smugglers were going, then they come into the building underground, and they come out through the window of the basement on the other side. And the little boys, neighborhood children, they used to give them some money to keep quiet. When I came out, I had no money to give at all. Is this on the Yom Kippur day, when you went to visit your sister's friend? Or is this when-- how you left the ghetto, when you went underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went to-- that time I went to see, I think, my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the basement. And I came to her, and I, and I said, do you know what day is today? She didn't know. And I said-- she said let's-- you bring me something, let's sit down and eat. I say, no, today's Yom Kippur. I don't eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we visited. And the women in whose place she was, had a-- she had a little boy. And she didn't want the woman to be present when I'm talking with my sister. So she sent her out for a walk with the child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then next time, when I went for good, I left. And I knew that my friend-- my sister's friend-- I had her telephone. But I didn't know the neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you decided to leave the ghetto, when was that when you decided to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I had to save the children. Because of them, they died and I'm alive. I decided to save the children, because the mother obligated me to save the children. That's why-- I didn't intend to. When I came to the ghetto, to the gate, there was a German guard, [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] and, and I had in my mind the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing would stop me. I'll go through fire and water-- nothing. So I'm at the gate, and that German-- God help him where he is, God bless him-- and I had $2, a silver coin. I gave it to him, and he let me out. Then I decided, where am I? I'm going to go. And I saw, on the horizon-- Excuse me, what happened to the children? You came out because of children. You never found the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, by then they were already killed. They took them to Majdenak and they shot them dead. How do I know? [? Mayer, ?] the one that used to be with the family, he lives-- he's alive. He's in Tel Aviv now. I don't know his last name. I would look for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, so you, you were out of the ghetto. And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, but I didn't tell you that when they kept shooting, [? Mayer ?] would pull at the daughter, whose husband died of typhus that time. So she was single, so to say, or alone. And the two of them, when they took them to Majdenak, they hid maybe bushes. And when the sun went down and started to be darkish, then they left that place. And she said to him, [? Mayer, ?] you are alone, I am alone. Let's get married. He said, Miss Paula, you are a lady and I am a servant. She was older, and he was much younger. So, she was in Warsaw when she met a Soviet general. And they got married, and he took her away to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you? Tell me what happened to you when you left the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: To me? Wait a minute. Before I left the ghetto, as I told you, the city was bombed much. And there was a wall standing of that building, a part of a building. And they were sitting the-- their sniper on it. And when you would pass by, he would target you. That time I was standing on the balcony when I saw a teenager with her coat like this, walking. And I, I saw smoke whirling around her. She fell, and I saw smoke when the bullet-- I didn't know when the bullet exploded, it gives you a smoke. I didn't know that. I saw that smoke.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those years they had no-- the transportation was a rickshaw. You know what's a rickshaw? Somebody took her. And I'm on the balcony, I see all this. They put her in the rickshaw, and then word came that she died on the, on the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, as for me, [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I had in my mind to save those children. I forgot to tell you, when they had thrown into the people into synagogues and everything, and they made boards-- [NON-ENGLISH], called [NON-ENGLISH] in Polish. Like a bunk bed. I don't know what you call it. And I saw with my-- and I was a visiting nurse to them. And I saw one sister-- two girls, maybe 11 and 10, maybe. I don't know now. She was-- the hunger, she grabbed her sister and bit her. She wanted to eat. That picture is always in me. Now, I was in charge of two orphanages, because the parents died long ago and they put them together. They were in separate streets. I walked. I still had my original clothing. I was dressed decently, and had a hat on my head and all. And on the way came those [NON-ENGLISH]. And they took me-- not only me, others. In Warsaw, the buildings are-- they have a big gate to get into the building. And they opened that gate. I don't know if it's a gate or something-- maybe you call it a gate. You can not see outside. It's not like-- it's a wall, it's a door, a big door. And they put me there. The reason, I don't know. And every time the door opens and they bring in others.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop now and change the tape.
